_about: Plain-English translations keyed by '<version>:<blockId>' (version-specific) or a bare '<blockId>' (shared across both tabs). AI-drafted from the Parliamentary Library Bills Digest and the text itself — verify before relying on them. Built incrementally; see NOTES.md for priority order.
original:s29-b1: Lists the situations in which someone stops being an NDIS participant.
original:s29-b3: The person moves permanently into residential aged care, or starts permanent home care, and this first happens after they turn 65.
original:s29-b4: The CEO formally revokes the person's participant status under the revocation rules in sections 30 and 30A.
original:s29-b5: The person tells the CEO in writing that they no longer want to be a participant. Under the original Act this took effect immediately — there was no cooling-off period and no safeguards around the decision.
original:s29-b6: These aged-care terms carry the same meaning they have in the Aged Care Act 1997.
original:s29-b7: Once someone stops being a participant, the NDIS will not pay for supports bought or provided after that point.
original:s29-b8: The NDIS rules can set out exactly when a support counts as bought or delivered before, versus after, someone leaves the scheme — which decides what still gets paid.
proposed:s29-b1: Lists the situations in which someone stops being an NDIS participant.
proposed:s29-b3: The person permanently starts ongoing funded aged-care services (other than a specialist aged-care program), first happening after they turn 65. The wording was modernised to match current aged-care terms.
proposed:s29-b4: The CEO formally revokes the person's participant status under the revocation rules in sections 30 and 30A.
proposed:s29-b5: CHANGED: the person is treated as having withdrawn under the new section 29A — i.e. they asked to leave and the 90-day cooling-off period ended without them cancelling. This replaces the old 'notify in writing and you're out immediately' rule with a safeguarded process.
proposed:s29-b6: Once someone stops being a participant, the NDIS will not pay for supports bought or provided after that point.
proposed:s29-b7: The NDIS rules can set out exactly when a support counts as bought or delivered before, versus after, someone leaves the scheme — which decides what still gets paid.
proposed:s29A-b1: NEW SECTION. A participant can ask to leave the NDIS, in whatever way the CEO has approved (for example, a form or an online process).
proposed:s29A-b2: Once the CEO receives that request, they must promptly send the person a written notice that has to include the things listed below.
proposed:s29A-b3: The notice must explain what leaving the NDIS would actually mean for the person.
proposed:s29A-b4: The notice must tell the person how to change their mind and cancel the request.
proposed:s29A-b5: The notice must explain that the person is only treated as having left once a set 'cooling-off period' ends — and only if they have not cancelled by then.
proposed:s29A-b6: The core new safeguard: the cooling-off period must be at least 90 days, counted from the day the notice is given — a guaranteed window to reconsider.
proposed:s29A-b7: The CEO can extend the cooling-off period, and can do so more than once, giving the person even longer to decide.
proposed:s29A-b8: If the person has not cancelled by the time the cooling-off period ends, that is the moment they are treated as having left the scheme — not before.
proposed:s29A-b9: Even if the person has a correspondence nominee who normally receives their mail, any notice about leaving the scheme must also go directly to the participant themselves.
proposed:s29A-b10: If the person has a plan nominee, that nominee must also be sent any notice about the person leaving — so the people supporting them are kept informed.
original:s73B-b1: The NDIS rules can require that certain kinds of supports may only be delivered by providers registered under section 73E.
original:s73B-b2: Cross-reference: where the Agency manages a plan's funding, supports must come from a registered provider (ss 32D(7) and 33(6)).
original:s73B-b3: Sets out when it is unlawful to provide a support under a participant's plan — both lettered conditions below must apply.
original:s73B-b4: The NDIS rules require the person to be registered to provide that support, and
original:s73B-b6: Under the original Act this was a civil penalty only — a maximum of 250 penalty units (about $82,500). There was no criminal offence, and no higher penalty for serious or systemic breaches.
proposed:s73B-b2: The NDIS rules can require that certain kinds of supports may only be delivered by providers registered under section 73E.
proposed:s73B-b3: Cross-reference: where the Agency manages a plan's funding, supports must come from a registered provider (ss 32D(7) and 33(6)).
proposed:s73B-b5: Defines the core breach — providing a support that requires registration without being registered. Each lettered point below must apply.
proposed:s73B-b6: The person actually provides a support to someone, and
proposed:s73B-b7: that support is provided under an NDIS participant's plan, and
proposed:s73B-b8: the NDIS rules require registration to provide that support, and
proposed:s73B-b11: A separate new breach: even just claiming you can provide a registration-required support, when you are not registered, is now unlawful — before you provide anything.
proposed:s73B-b12: The person presents themselves as able to provide a support under a plan, and
proposed:s73B-b13: the NDIS rules require registration for that support, and
proposed:s73B-b15: 'Fault-based offence' — the most serious tier: generally the prosecution must prove the person meant to do it or was reckless. NEW — there was no criminal offence here at all under the original Act.
proposed:s73B-b16: Breaching (2) or (3) is now a criminal offence. 'Absolute liability' on the rule-requirement elements means it is no defence to say you did not know the rules required registration.
proposed:s73B-b17: NEW criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, a fine of up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
proposed:s73B-b18: 'Strict liability offence' — a lower criminal tier: the prosecution does not have to prove intent, so it is quicker to enforce.
proposed:s73B-b19: The same conduct is also a strict-liability criminal offence — no need to prove the provider meant to break the rule.
proposed:s73B-b20: Penalty for the strict-liability offence: up to 60 penalty units (about $19,800).
proposed:s73B-b21: 'Civil penalty' — pursued in court like a fine, without the criminal burden of proof. This pathway existed before, but the amounts are now far higher for serious breaches.
proposed:s73B-b22: Breaching (2) or (3) also exposes the person to a civil penalty.
proposed:s73B-b23: The civil penalty amount now depends on how serious the breach is —
proposed:s73B-b24: For a serious contravention by an NDIS provider: up to 10,000 penalty units — about $3.3 million. This is the major escalation; the old maximum was 250 units.
proposed:s73B-b25: For any other breach: up to 250 penalty units (about $82,500) — the same as the old maximum.
s73ZN-b1: Banning orders aimed at NDIS providers — current or former.
s73ZN-b2: The NDIS Commissioner can issue a 'banning order' that stops or limits what an NDIS provider (current or former) is allowed to do, on any of the grounds listed below.
s73ZN-b12: Banning orders aimed at the workers and key personnel of NDIS providers.
s73ZN-b13: The Commissioner can also ban an individual worker, contractor, or key person of an NDIS provider from specified activities, on the grounds listed below.
s73ZN-b21: Banning orders aimed at people who have never been in the sector but are found unsuitable.
s73ZN-b22: The Commissioner can ban someone from working with people with disability even if they have never been an NDIS provider or worker — where they are found unsuitable and the conditions below are met.
proposed:s73ZN-b27: NEW (item 80): a whole new category of banning order — reaching registration applicants, quality auditors, and the people and services around them.
proposed:s73ZN-b28: NEW: the Commissioner can ban any of the people listed in subsection (2C) below from specified activities, on any of the grounds that follow.
proposed:s73ZN-b29: their registration as an NDIS provider has been revoked, or
proposed:s73ZN-b30: their approval to act as a quality auditor has been revoked, or
proposed:s73ZN-b31: the Commissioner reasonably believes one of the following —
proposed:s73ZN-b32: they have broken, are breaking, or are likely to break the Act, or
proposed:s73ZN-b33: they have been, or are likely to be, involved in someone else breaking the Act, or
proposed:s73ZN-b34: for the facilitation or advisory people in (2C)(c)–(d): that they are not suitable to be involved in that service, or
proposed:s73ZN-b35: they are convicted of an offence involving fraud or dishonesty, or
proposed:s73ZN-b37: NEW: defines exactly who the new (2B) banning power can reach —
proposed:s73ZN-b38: anyone with a pending application to become a registered NDIS provider — they can be banned before their application is even decided;
proposed:s73ZN-b39: approved quality auditors — the bodies that audit providers for registration;
proposed:s73ZN-b40: the employees and key personnel of those applicants and auditors;
proposed:s73ZN-b41: and people providing the kinds of support services listed below —
proposed:s73ZN-b42: services that enable or facilitate disability supports — a broad reach into the surrounding market;
proposed:s73ZN-b43: in particular, registration consultants — people who help with or advise on provider-registration applications under section 73C.
original:s73ZN-b47: Under the original Act, breaching a banning order was dealt with here, as a civil penalty.
original:s73ZN-b48: Sets out the breach: engaging in conduct that breaches a banning order made against the person.
original:s73ZN-b50: that conduct breaches a banning order made against them (including one of its conditions).
original:s73ZN-b51: CHANGED: under the original Act this was a civil penalty only — up to 1,000 penalty units (about $330,000). The 2026 Act repeals this subsection and replaces it with the new section 73ZNA, which adds criminal offences — up to 5 years' imprisonment — on top of a much larger civil penalty.
proposed:s73ZOA-b1: NEW SECTION. The Commissioner's new power to make 'anti-promotion orders'.
proposed:s73ZOA-b2: NEW POWER: the Commissioner can order a person to stop or limit specified 'regulated promotional conduct', once satisfied the person has engaged or is engaging in it. Aimed at predatory marketing that exploits participants and damages the scheme.
proposed:s73ZOA-b3: What counts as 'regulated promotional conduct' is set by the NDIS rules — covering the promotion, advertising or marketing of:
proposed:s73ZOA-b4: supports or services for people with disability; or
proposed:s73ZOA-b6: things that claim — openly or by implication — to be the things in (a) or (b).
proposed:s73ZOA-b7: A safeguard on the rule-making: the Minister can only prescribe conduct as 'regulated' if satisfied it undermines the Act's objects or the section 4 principles.
proposed:s73ZOA-b8: How an anti-promotion order can be framed.
proposed:s73ZOA-b10: broad or narrow in scope; and
proposed:s73ZOA-b12: permanent, or for a set period.
proposed:s73ZOA-b13: When the order starts — no earlier than:
proposed:s73ZOA-b14: normally, 7 days after the order is given to the person; or
proposed:s73ZOA-b15: if urgent protection of a person with disability requires it, the very next day after the order is given.
proposed:s73ZOA-b16: These orders are not legislative instruments — they are not subject to the usual parliamentary tabling and disallowance process.
proposed:s73ZOA-b17: The process the Commissioner must follow before making an order.
proposed:s73ZOA-b18: An order can only be made if both of the following happen first —
proposed:s73ZOA-b19: the person is given a chance to put their case to the Commissioner; and
proposed:s73ZOA-b20: at least 7 days have passed since that chance was given.
proposed:s73ZOA-b21: Once an order is made, the Commissioner must —
proposed:s73ZOA-b22: give the order to the person it is made against; and
proposed:s73ZOA-b23: include written reasons for the order.
proposed:s73ZOB-b1: NEW SECTION. Sets out the breach — failing to comply with an anti-promotion order.
proposed:s73ZOB-b2: an anti-promotion order has been made against the person, and
proposed:s73ZOB-b4: that conduct breaches the order (or one of its conditions).
proposed:s73ZOB-b5: Breaching an anti-promotion order is a civil penalty — up to 250 penalty units (about $82,500).
proposed:s73ZOC-b1: NEW SECTION. How an anti-promotion order can be changed or cancelled.
proposed:s73ZOC-b2: The Commissioner can change or cancel an anti-promotion order, by written notice, whenever satisfied it is appropriate.
proposed:s73ZOC-b4: on the Commissioner's own initiative; or
proposed:s73ZOC-b5: at the request of the person the order is against.
proposed:s73ZOC-b6: A variation can add, change, or remove conditions.
proposed:s73ZOC-b7: The process for a person's request to change or cancel an order.
proposed:s73ZOC-b8: A request by the affected person must —
proposed:s73ZOC-b10: use any form the Commissioner has approved; and
proposed:s73ZOC-b11: include whatever information and documents the Commissioner requires.
proposed:s73ZOC-b12: If the Commissioner intends to refuse the person's request, they must —
proposed:s73ZOC-b13: give the person a chance to make submissions; and
proposed:s73ZOC-b14: wait at least 7 days after giving that chance before deciding.
proposed:s73ZOC-b15: The notice changing or cancelling an order must include written reasons.
proposed:s73ZOC-b16: When a variation or revocation takes effect.
proposed:s73ZOC-b17: A variation starts no earlier than —
proposed:s73ZOC-b18: normally, 7 days after the notice is given; or
proposed:s73ZOC-b19: if urgent protection of a person with disability requires it, the next day after the notice.
proposed:s73ZOC-b20: A revocation (cancellation) starts no earlier than the day the notice is given.
proposed:s73ZOC-b21: Like the orders themselves, variations and revocations are not legislative instruments.
proposed:s59A-b1: NEW SECTION. A new breach — knowingly giving the NDIS Commission false or misleading information.
proposed:s59A-b2: the person gives information or a document to the Commissioner or a Commission officer for a purpose under the Act, and
proposed:s59A-b3: the person knows it is false or misleading in a way that matters.
proposed:s59A-b4: Cross-reference: deliberately false statements can also be criminal offences under Part 7.4 of the Criminal Code — this section adds a civil pathway alongside that.
proposed:s59A-b5: Civil penalty — up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600). A civil pathway lets the Commission act without meeting the criminal burden of proof.
proposed:s73ZNA-b1: NEW SECTION (replaces the repealed s73ZN(10)). Sets out the breach — doing something that breaches a banning order made against you.
proposed:s73ZNA-b2: a banning order has been made against the person, and
proposed:s73ZNA-b4: that conduct breaches the banning order (or one of its conditions).
proposed:s73ZNA-b5: 'Fault-based offence' — the most serious tier: generally the prosecution must prove intent or recklessness.
proposed:s73ZNA-b6: NEW: breaching a banning order is now a criminal offence — it was civil-penalty-only under the original Act.
proposed:s73ZNA-b7: NEW criminal penalty: up to 5 years' imprisonment, a fine of up to 300 penalty units (about $99,000), or both.
proposed:s73ZNA-b8: 'Strict liability offence' — a lower criminal tier: no need to prove intent, so quicker to enforce.
proposed:s73ZNA-b9: The same conduct is also a strict-liability criminal offence.
proposed:s73ZNA-b10: Penalty for the strict-liability offence: up to 150 penalty units (about $49,500).
proposed:s73ZNA-b11: 'Civil penalty' — court-imposed, without the criminal burden of proof.
proposed:s73ZNA-b12: Breaching a banning order also exposes the person to a civil penalty.
proposed:s73ZNA-b13: The civil penalty depends on how serious the breach is —
proposed:s73ZNA-b14: for a serious contravention by an NDIS provider: up to 10,000 penalty units (about $3.3 million); or
proposed:s73ZNA-b15: for any other breach: up to 1,000 penalty units (about $330,000) — the same as the old s73ZN(10) maximum.
proposed:s73ZBA-b1: NEW SECTION (whistleblower protection). Makes it a breach to reveal the identity of someone who has made a protected disclosure.
proposed:s73ZBA-b2: someone (the 'discloser') has made a disclosure that qualifies for whistleblower protection under this Division, and
proposed:s73ZBA-b3: the person reveals either of the following —
proposed:s73ZBA-b5: or information likely to identify them; and
proposed:s73ZBA-b6: they obtained that information because of the disclosure (directly or indirectly), and
proposed:s73ZBA-b7: and the way they reveal it —
proposed:s73ZBA-b8: is to someone other than the discloser, and
proposed:s73ZBA-b9: is not one of the authorised exceptions in subsection (2).
proposed:s73ZBA-b10: Civil penalty for outing a whistleblower — up to 30 penalty units (about $9,900).
proposed:s73ZBA-b11: The exceptions — revealing a discloser's identity is allowed only if one of these applies:
proposed:s73ZBA-b12: it goes to the NDIS Commissioner or the Agency; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b13: it goes to any of these law-enforcement people —
proposed:s73ZBA-b14: an Australian Federal Police member; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b16: a State or Territory police officer; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b18: it goes to a lawyer, to get legal advice or representation about this Division; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b19: the discloser consents, or has already acted in a way inconsistent with staying anonymous; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b20: it is necessary to lessen or prevent a serious threat to someone's safety, health or wellbeing; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b21: it goes to a court, tribunal or Royal Commission; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b22: the discloser has chosen to have it handled as a complaint, and the disclosure is for running that complaint; or
proposed:s73ZBA-b24: the information was already public beforehand, and
proposed:s73ZBA-b25: it became public lawfully — not because someone else breached this section.
proposed:s73ZBA-b26: Cross-reference: the NDIS rules can set out how complaints are made, managed and resolved (s73X).
proposed:s73ZBA-b27: A further carve-out — the breach does not apply where:
proposed:s73ZBA-b29: is not the discloser's identity itself, and
proposed:s73ZBA-b30: is reasonably necessary to deal with the wrongdoing the disclosure points to, and
proposed:s73ZBA-b31: the person takes all reasonable steps to reduce the risk the discloser is identified.
proposed:s73ZBA-b32: Defines 'prosecutor' for this section: someone who starts or runs a prosecution of an offence.
original:s67B-b1: Criminal offence — recording, disclosing or otherwise using protected NDIS Commission information without authorisation.
original:s67B-b6: the recording, disclosure or use is not authorised or required under the Act, and
original:s67B-b7: the information is protected Commission information.
original:s67B-b8: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
original:s67B-b9: For a company convicted of this offence, a court can impose a fine up to 5 times the amount above. The 2026 Act removes this note (the multiplier still applies generally under the Crimes Act).
proposed:s67B-b1: The criminal offence pathway — unchanged in substance. The 2026 Act adds a civil pathway below.
proposed:s67B-b2: Criminal offence — recording, disclosing or otherwise using protected Commission information without authorisation.
proposed:s67B-b7: the recording, disclosure or use is not authorised or required under the Act, and
proposed:s67B-b8: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67B-b9: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
proposed:s67B-b10: NEW: a civil penalty pathway for the same conduct — letting the Commission enforce without meeting the criminal burden of proof.
proposed:s67B-b11: NEW civil penalty — the same misuse of protected Commission information, pursued civilly.
proposed:s67B-b16: the recording, disclosure or use is not authorised or required under the Act, and
proposed:s67B-b17: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67B-b18: NEW civil penalty: up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600).
original:s67C-b1: Criminal offence — asking someone to disclose protected NDIS Commission information.
original:s67C-b2: the person asks a Commission officer or anyone else to disclose information — whether or not anything is actually disclosed, and
original:s67C-b3: that disclosure would breach this Part, and
original:s67C-b4: the information is protected Commission information.
original:s67C-b5: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
original:s67C-b6: For a company convicted, a court can impose a fine up to 5 times the amount above. The 2026 Act removes this note.
proposed:s67C-b1: The criminal offence pathway. The 2026 Act adds a civil pathway below.
proposed:s67C-b2: Criminal offence — soliciting the disclosure of protected Commission information.
proposed:s67C-b3: the person asks a Commission officer or anyone else to disclose information — whether or not anything is actually disclosed, and
proposed:s67C-b4: that disclosure would breach this Part, and
proposed:s67C-b5: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67C-b6: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
proposed:s67C-b7: NEW: a civil penalty pathway for soliciting protected information.
proposed:s67C-b8: NEW civil penalty — the same soliciting conduct, pursued civilly.
proposed:s67C-b9: the person asks a Commission officer or anyone else to disclose information — whether or not anything is actually disclosed, and
proposed:s67C-b10: that disclosure would breach this Part, and
proposed:s67C-b11: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67C-b12: NEW civil penalty: up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600).
original:s67D-b1: Criminal offence — offering to supply protected Commission information about someone.
original:s67D-b2: the person offers to supply information about another person, and
original:s67D-b3: the person knows it is protected Commission information.
original:s67D-b4: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
original:s67D-b5: For a company convicted, a court can fine up to 5 times the amount above. The 2026 Act removes this note.
original:s67D-b6: A second criminal offence — holding yourself out as able to supply protected Commission information.
original:s67D-b7: the person presents themselves as able to supply information about another person, and
original:s67D-b8: the person knows it is protected Commission information.
original:s67D-b9: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
original:s67D-b10: For a company convicted, a court can fine up to 5 times the amount above. The 2026 Act removes this note.
original:s67D-b11: Exception: none of this catches people acting in the course of their lawful duties, functions or powers under the Act.
proposed:s67D-b1: The criminal offence of offering to supply protected information.
proposed:s67D-b2: Criminal offence — offering to supply protected Commission information about someone.
proposed:s67D-b3: the person offers to supply information about another person, and
proposed:s67D-b4: the person knows it is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67D-b5: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
proposed:s67D-b6: NEW: a civil penalty pathway for offering to supply protected information.
proposed:s67D-b7: NEW civil penalty — offering to supply protected information, pursued civilly. Unlike the criminal offence, this does not require proving the person knew the information was protected.
proposed:s67D-b8: the person offers to supply information about another person, and
proposed:s67D-b9: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67D-b10: NEW civil penalty: up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600).
proposed:s67D-b11: The criminal offence of holding yourself out as able to supply protected information.
proposed:s67D-b12: Criminal offence — holding yourself out as able to supply protected Commission information.
proposed:s67D-b13: the person presents themselves as able to supply information about another person, and
proposed:s67D-b14: the person knows it is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67D-b15: Criminal penalty: up to 2 years' imprisonment, 120 penalty units (about $39,600), or both.
proposed:s67D-b16: NEW: a civil penalty pathway for holding yourself out as able to supply protected information.
proposed:s67D-b17: NEW civil penalty — holding yourself out as able to supply protected information, pursued civilly (no need to prove knowledge).
proposed:s67D-b18: the person presents themselves as able to supply information about another person, and
proposed:s67D-b19: the information is protected Commission information.
proposed:s67D-b20: NEW civil penalty: up to 120 penalty units (about $39,600).
proposed:s67D-b21: The exception for people doing their lawful job.
proposed:s67D-b22: Exception: none of these offences or civil penalties catch people acting in the course of their lawful duties, functions or powers under the Act.
proposed:s67D-b23: If relying on that exception, the person must point to evidence supporting it (an evidential burden) — see Criminal Code s13.3(3) and Regulatory Powers Act s96.
s56-b1: An information-gathering requirement (under s55 or s55A) must be made in a written notice given to the person.
s56-b2: The notice must spell out the following —
s56-b3: what information or document is required;
s56-b4: how the person is to provide it;
s56-b6: if the notice is from the CEO, which Agency officer to give it to;
s56-b7: if the notice is from the Commissioner, which Commission officer to give it to;
s56-b8: and a statement that the notice is given under this section.
original:s56-b9: The deadline must be at least 14 days from the day the notice is given. Under the original Act this minimum was fixed — there was no power to shorten it.
original:s56-b10: Where the CEO gives the notice —
original:s56-b11: it can require the person to appear before a named Agency officer and answer questions;
original:s56-b12: and it must set a time and place at least 14 days after the notice is given.
original:s56-b13: Where the Commissioner gives the notice —
original:s56-b14: it can require the person to appear before a named Commission officer and answer questions;
original:s56-b15: and it must set a time and place at least 14 days after the notice is given.
proposed:s56-b9: CHANGED: the 14-day minimum deadline still applies — but for a notice from the Commissioner it can now be shortened under the new subsection (6).
proposed:s56-b10: When a notice can compel the person to appear in person and answer questions.
proposed:s56-b11: Where the CEO gives the notice —
proposed:s56-b12: it can require the person to appear before a named Agency officer and answer questions;
proposed:s56-b13: and it must set a time and place at least 14 days after the notice is given.
proposed:s56-b14: Where the Commissioner gives the notice —
proposed:s56-b15: it can require the person to appear before a named Commission officer and answer questions;
proposed:s56-b16: and it must set a time and place at least 14 days out — but, for a Commissioner's notice, that can be brought forward under subsection (6).
proposed:s56-b17: NEW: the Commissioner's power to compress the deadline in urgent cases.
proposed:s56-b18: NEW: if all of the subsection (7) conditions are met, a notice from the Commissioner may set —
proposed:s56-b19: a deadline shorter than 14 days; or
proposed:s56-b20: an appearance time sooner than 14 days.
proposed:s56-b21: NEW: the conditions that must ALL be met before the timeframe can be shortened —
proposed:s56-b22: the notice is from the Commissioner (not the CEO);
proposed:s56-b23: the Commissioner reasonably believes the normal timeframe would significantly increase the risk of serious harm to a participant; and
proposed:s56-b24: the shorter timeframe is reasonable in the circumstances.
s57-b1: It is an offence to refuse or fail to comply with an information-gathering requirement under s55 or s55A.
s57-b2: Criminal penalty: up to 30 penalty units (about $9,900).
original:s57-b3: For a company convicted of this offence, a court can impose a fine up to 5 times the amount above. The 2026 Act removes this note.
original:s57-b4: There is no offence if the person has a reasonable excuse.
original:s57-b5: To rely on the reasonable-excuse defence, the person must point to evidence supporting it (an evidential burden).

proposed:s11B-b1: NEW SECTION. Defines a 'serious contravention' — the trigger for the much higher penalties throughout the bill. A contravention is serious if the conduct —
proposed:s11B-b3: is part of a 'systematic pattern of conduct'.
proposed:s11B-b5: A 'significant failure' is conduct that departs significantly from what could reasonably be expected of an NDIS provider or its key personnel, judged against the requirements the Act places on —
proposed:s11B-b6: NDIS providers and their key personnel generally;
proposed:s11B-b7: and, for a registered provider, the (higher) requirements on registered providers and their key personnel.
proposed:s11B-b9: Whether conduct is part of a 'systematic pattern' is judged on —
proposed:s11B-b10: how many times the provider or key person has breached the Act;
proposed:s11B-b11: the period over which those breaches occurred;
proposed:s11B-b13: and how the provider or key person responded — or failed to respond — to complaints about them.
proposed:s73ZKA-b1: NEW SECTION. If a regulator seeks a civil penalty order for a 'serious contravention', the application must specifically identify that serious contravention.
proposed:s73ZKA-b2: In a serious-contravention civil penalty case, if the court —
proposed:s73ZKA-b3: is not satisfied a serious contravention occurred; but
proposed:s73ZKA-b4: is satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the (ordinary) provision was contravened;
proposed:s73ZKA-b5: — the court can still make a civil penalty order for the ordinary contravention. A serious-contravention claim doesn't fail entirely just because 'serious' isn't proven.
proposed:s199A-b1: NEW SECTION. Applies where one provision says that contravening another provision (the 'conduct rule provision') is an offence.
proposed:s199A-b2: In that case, the physical elements of the offence are taken to be set out in the conduct rule provision — a drafting mechanism so the new split offence/penalty structure works under the Criminal Code.
proposed:s199A-b3: Note: Chapter 2 of the Criminal Code sets out the general principles of criminal responsibility.
proposed:s199B-b1: NEW SECTION. Applies where one provision says that contravening another (the 'conduct provision') is an offence or attracts a civil penalty.
proposed:s199B-b2: In that case, a reference to contravening the offence or civil penalty provision also covers contravening the underlying conduct provision — making the split offence/penalty structure hang together.
proposed:s199B-b3: Note: this also flows through to references in the Regulatory Powers Act.
proposed:s199C-b1: NEW SECTION. In the proceedings listed below, a certificate signed by the Commissioner is prima facie evidence of what it states — an evidentiary shortcut that saves proving routine registration facts from scratch.
proposed:s199C-b3: Criminal Code fraud or false-statement proceedings relating to the Commission;
proposed:s199C-b5: The certificate can state any of the following —
proposed:s199C-b6: when the Commissioner received a person's registration application;
proposed:s199C-b7: when the Commissioner registered a person as an NDIS provider;
proposed:s199C-b8: when a person transitioned across from the old registered-provider scheme (2017 Act);
proposed:s199C-b9: when a person's registration will cease;
proposed:s199C-b10: when a person's registration ceased or was revoked;
proposed:s199C-b11: the classes of supports or services a provider is registered for;
proposed:s199C-b12: when a variation of a person's registration takes effect;
proposed:s199C-b13: when the Commissioner decided to vary or revoke a registration;
proposed:s199C-b14: and any other matter prescribed by the NDIS rules.
